Why Foraging Toys Are Essential for Your Parrot's Mental Health

Here's a number that should change how you feed your parrot: a wild African grey parrot spends 4-6 hours per day foraging for food. A wild cockatoo covers 10-20 miles of flight daily while locating food sources. A wild macaw manipulates dozens of different fruit types, nuts with varying shell hardness, and seed pods that require specific techniques to open. Now compare that to a domestic parrot: a bowl of pellets is placed in front of them twice a day, and the entire food-finding process takes approximately 30 seconds. The gap between what a parrot's brain evolved to do and what captivity provides is the single largest welfare problem in companion parrot ownership.

This gap manifests as what avian behaviorists call "foraging deprivation syndrome" — a spectrum of behavioral and psychological issues that develop when intelligent birds lack adequate mental stimulation. Feather-destructive behavior (feather plucking, chewing, barbering) is the most visible symptom, affecting an estimated 30-50% of captive parrots at some point in their lives. But there's more: chronic screaming (contact calls amplified by boredom), aggression toward humans and other birds, stereotypic behaviors (head-turning, pacing, repetitive vocalizations), and self-mutilation in severe cases. Every single one of these behaviors decreases significantly when foraging enrichment is introduced — dozens of peer-reviewed studies have demonstrated this across multiple parrot species.

Foraging toys work by tapping into the natural behaviors that parrots evolved over millions of years: searching, manipulating, problem-solving, and extracting food rewards. A foraging toy doesn't just provide food — it provides the process of finding food. The bird must tear, shred, unscrew, slide, or puzzle through obstacles to reach the reward. This process engages multiple cognitive systems simultaneously: visual search (finding the toy), motor planning (figuring out the manipulation sequence), fine motor control (executing precise movements), and reward evaluation (learning which strategies work). A parrot that spends 30 minutes working on a complex foraging toy has exercised its brain more than a parrot that eats from a bowl for 30 seconds.

The benefits extend beyond behavior. Foraging toys slow eating rate (pellets consumed in 30 seconds vs. over 30 minutes = better digestion and reduced crop issues). They provide physical exercise (manipulating toys strengthens beak and foot muscles). They increase the time budget (occupied birds are quieter birds — a foraging parrot doesn't have time to scream). And they build confidence — parrots that successfully solve foraging puzzles show measurable reductions in stress hormones and increased exploratory behavior in novel environments.

This guide covers the best bird foraging toys and puzzle feeders for every species and skill level: beginner toys for birds new to foraging, intermediate puzzles for experienced foragers, heavy-duty toys for large powerful parrots, and DIY-foraging concepts for creative owners.

Understanding Foraging Difficulty Levels: Match the Toy to Your Bird

  • Level 1 — Beginner (Easy): For birds that have never experienced foraging, the goal is to teach the concept that effort leads to reward. Level 1 toys involve minimal obstruction: food hidden in a visible, easily accessible location covered by a single layer of paper or a loose flap. Examples: tissue paper wrapped loosely around a favorite treat, a simple bowl covered with a single sheet of paper, foraging wheels with obvious food-visible pockets. Parrots should succeed at Level 1 within 1-3 attempts, building confidence to try harder challenges
  • Level 2 — Intermediate (Moderate): Once the bird understands foraging, Level 2 toys require multiple actions to access food: pulling a paper strip to release a hidden nut, pushing a sliding door to reveal a seed compartment, or turning a wooden knob to open a small drawer. These toys require fine motor control and understanding of cause-and-effect relationships. Most parrots adapt to Level 2 within 1-2 weeks and should be rotated between 3-4 different Level 2 toys to prevent habituation
  • Level 3 — Advanced (Hard): For experienced foragers, Level 3 toys require sequences of actions in the correct order: unscrew a plastic bolt, lift a latch, slide a door, extract a drawer, and remove the food item. These multi-step puzzles engage working memory and planning skills — the bird must remember which step comes next and execute the sequence correctly. Advanced toys also include destructive foraging (shredding through cardboard, wood, or palm leaf to reach hidden food) which satisfies the natural urge to chew and destroy
  • Level 4 — Expert (Very Hard): The most challenging foragers — large macaws, cockatoos, and African greys — need puzzle boxes with multiple sequential locks, combination-style puzzles where specific actions must be performed in specific sequences, and foraging mazes that require the bird to move the toy itself to direct food toward an exit hole. These toys provide 30-60+ minutes of engagement per session and should be used as part of the daily feeding routine to ensure the bird works for a portion of its daily food intake

Top 8 Bird Foraging Toys & Puzzle Feeders for 2026

Bird foraging toys and puzzle feeders for mental enrichment

1. Planet Pleasures Foraging Wheel — Best Beginner Foraging Toy

The Planet Pleasures Foraging Wheel is the single best introductory foraging toy for parrots of all sizes. It's a simple concept done perfectly: a wooden wheel with multiple compartments around the perimeter, each covered by a sliding paper or wooden cover. Food visible through small viewing holes attracts the bird's attention. The bird then pulls or shreds the cover to access the compartment. The wheel rotates, presenting new compartments as the bird works through the toy. The entire toy is made from sustainably harvested sola wood (a soft, fast-growing wood that's safe for birds to chew and destroy) and natural vegetable dyes.

The Foraging Wheel works for species from budgies and cockatiels (the small version) through African greys and Amazons (the large version). The sola wood construction means the toy is almost entirely consumable — the bird destroys it over weeks of use, and when it's gone, you buy a replacement. This is actually a feature, not a drawback: destructive foraging (shredding to reach food) is the most natural foraging behavior for parrots, and the Wheel satisfies this drive completely. The toy is sized proportionally to the bird: budgies get a 4-inch wheel, macaws get an 8-inch wheel with thicker compartments that require more effort to shred.

Pros:

  • Perfect beginner foraging toy — the concept is immediately intuitive (food is visible, just needs uncovering)
  • Made from sola wood — safe to chew and destroy, sustainably harvested, vegetable-dyed colors
  • Multiple compartments encourage sequential foraging — the bird works through each one
  • Available in multiple sizes for budgies through macaws
  • Entirely consumable — no plastic parts that could break off and be ingested
  • Promotes destructive foraging behavior (shredding) — the most natural foraging type
  • Inexpensive — $8-15 depending on size, replacement cost is reasonable

Cons:

  • Single-use — once destroyed, it's gone (buy replacements in bulk to save)
  • Not suitable as a daily feeder — it's a one-time enrichment toy, not a food delivery system
  • Small pieces of sola wood can scatter around the cage when shredded
  • Some birds figure it out quickly and destroy the entire toy in one session
  • The paper covers can be removed without shredding by smarter birds who figure out the mechanism

Rating: 4.5/5 | Best For: Beginners new to foraging, budgies, cockatiels, conures, and small-to-medium parrots

2. Nina Ottosson for Birds Puzzle Box — Best Multi-Step Puzzle Feeder

Nina Ottosson is the world leader in pet puzzle toys — their dog puzzles are legendary, and their bird-specific line brings the same Scandinavian design philosophy to parrot enrichment. The Puzzle Box is a wooden box with multiple sliding drawers, rotating discs, and hinged lids — each requiring a different manipulation technique to open. One drawer slides sideways. Another requires lifting a wooden knob and pivoting it. A third has a rotating top that must be turned to align food access holes. The bird must solve all three locking mechanisms in sequence to access all compartments.

The Puzzle Box is made from birch plywood (sanded smooth, no splinters) with safe water-based stains — no paints or varnishes that could be toxic. The difficulty level is solidly at Level 3 (Advanced) — most birds need 2-5 sessions to figure out all three locking mechanisms. Once solved, the bird can complete the puzzle in 30-60 seconds, so it's important to rotate the Puzzle Box with other toys to prevent over-familiarity. The compartments hold a tablespoon of pellets, chopped nuts, or seed mix — enough for a portion of the bird's daily diet. For maximum engagement, use the Puzzle Box as the bird's first meal of the day (morning feeding), when parrots are most motivated to forage.

Pros:

  • Three different locking mechanisms — sliding drawer, rotating disc, hinged lid — in one toy
  • High-quality birch plywood construction with child-safe water-based stains
  • Teaches multi-step problem-solving — the bird must understand sequences of actions
  • Holds a substantial portion of daily food (1-2 tablespoons)
  • Easy to clean — hand wash with mild soap, air dry
  • Durable — lasts months to years of daily use (not a consumable toy)
  • Can be disassembled to adjust difficulty (remove some locking mechanisms)
  • $25-35 — excellent value for a durable, multi-year foraging tool

Cons:

  • Advanced difficulty — may frustrate beginners who haven't learned foraging basics
  • Some birds learn the sequence quickly and solve it in 30 seconds — rotation is essential
  • Wood construction can splinter if the bird chews aggressively on edges (supervise heavy chewers)
  • Not suitable for very large macaws or cockatoos — the wood isn't thick enough for powerful beaks
  • Drawers can be chewed on rather than slid open — some birds never learn the sliding mechanism

Rating: 4.5/5 | Best For: Intermediate-to-advanced foragers, African greys, Amazons, mini-macaws, cockatoos (medium sizes)

3. Super Bird Creations Foraging Box — Best Shredding Foraging Toy

Super Bird Creations makes the most durable foraging toys for large, destructive parrots — and the Foraging Box is their flagship product. It's a clear acrylic box (virtually indestructible for most parrots — they can see the food but can't bite through the acrylic) with a paper-based shredding insert on the top. The bird must shred through the paper insert to access the food chamber below. When the paper is destroyed, you insert a new paper refill (sold in packs of 12, $8-10). The design is genius in its simplicity: it satisfies the shredding drive of large parrots while protecting the actual toy structure from destruction.

The Foraging Box comes in two sizes: Medium (for conures, Senegal parrots, caiques) and Large (for African greys, Amazons, cockatoos, and macaws). The Large version holds up to 1/4 cup of food per loading — enough for a single meal. The acrylic is thick enough that even a determined macaw can't crack it (anecdotal evidence from owners of 30+ years with the same toy). The paper inserts vary in thickness — some are single-layer (easier), some are multi-layer with hidden compartments (harder) — letting you adjust difficulty as the bird gains experience.

Pros:

  • Indestructible acrylic outer box — survives even the most determined macaw and cockatoo
  • Replaceable paper inserts satisfy destructive foraging drive — the bird shreds the paper, not the toy
  • Refill packs are inexpensive ($8-10 for 12 inserts) — low ongoing cost
  • Clear acrylic lets the bird see the food — maintains motivation to shred through the paper
  • Large capacity (1/4 cup) — can serve as a meal feeder, not just a treat dispenser
  • Difficulty adjustable — use single-layer paper for beginners, multi-layer for experts
  • Dishwasher safe (acrylic top rack) for easy cleaning

Cons:

  • Limited foraging variety — it's shred-and-access only, no other manipulation challenges
  • Acrylic can scratch over time (cosmetic only — doesn't affect function)
  • Refills are consumable — you need to buy them regularly (though 12 inserts last a month with daily use)
  • More expensive initial purchase — $20-30 for the box plus ongoing refill costs
  • Some smarter birds learn to unscrew the side locks rather than shred the paper — defeating the purpose

Rating: 4.5/5 | Best For: Large destructive parrots (macaws, cockatoos, large Amazons), heavy shredders

4. ATOM Foraging Pinecone — Best Natural Foraging Toy

The ATOM Foraging Pinecone is the simplest foraging toy on this list — and one of the most effective. It's exactly what it sounds like: a real pinecone (collected sustainably, heat-treated to kill pests) with food items wedged between the scales. The bird must pick, pry, and manipulate each scale to extract the treat. This mimics the natural foraging behavior of wild parrots who spend hours extracting seeds from pinecones, bark crevices, and other natural structures. The pinecone itself is also a suitable chewing material — many birds will destroy the entire cone over several days.

To use: wedge nuts (almonds, pine nuts, walnuts), seeds, pellets, or dried fruit between the cone scales. Adjust difficulty by how deeply you wedge the food — shallow wedging for beginners, deep wedging between tightly closed scales for advanced foragers. Hang the pinecone from the cage top or attach it to a perch so the bird must work at it. The pinecone is 100% biodegradable and completely safe for birds to ingest small amounts of wood (it's indigestible but passes through harmlessly). Once the bird has destroyed the pinecone, simply buy a new one — they're sold in bulk packs of 6-12 for $10-15.

Pros:

  • Most natural foraging experience — mimics wild eating behavior exactly
  • 100% biodegradable, all-natural materials — zero plastic, zero synthetic components
  • Customizable difficulty — wedging treats deeper between tighter scales increases challenge
  • Engages the bird for 15-45 minutes depending on how many treats are wedged
  • Also serves as a chew toy — many birds destroy the cone itself for entertainment
  • Bulk packs make them affordable for multi-bird households
  • Works for all parrot sizes — small cones for budgies, large cones for macaws

Cons:

  • Single-use — the pinecone is destroyed during use, must be replaced
  • Can scatter pinecone debris and seed shells around the cage
  • Some birds are afraid of pinecones initially (unfamiliar texture/shape)
  • Pine sap residue can transfer to hands (the heat-treatment process removes most but not all sap)
  • Small cones may not hold enough food for large parrots' appetite

Rating: 4/5 | Best For: All parrot species, natural foraging enthusiasts, birds that love to destroy things

5. Kaboost Foraging Skewer — Best Foraging Toy for Fresh Foods

The Kaboost Foraging Skewer addresses a specific problem: getting parrots to eat fresh fruits and vegetables. Many parrot owners struggle to introduce healthy fresh foods because parrots are naturally neophobic (afraid of new things). A bird that won't touch a piece of broccoli in a bowl will frequently investigate and eat that same broccoli when it's skewered on a colorful, interesting toy. The Foraging Skewer is a stainless steel rod with food-holding prongs — you thread fresh foods onto the skewer (apple chunks, carrot sticks, bell pepper slices, corn on the cob, leafy greens) and hang it in the cage. The bird must manipulate the skewer — pulling food off the prongs, rotating it to access different pieces, and holding the skewer steady while removing food items.

The skewer design provides multiple benefits: it presents food in a novel, interesting way that overcomes neophobia; the manipulation required to remove food is itself foraging enrichment; and hanging the food at different heights in the cage encourages climbing and exercise. The stainless steel construction is hygienic, dishwasher-safe, and won't rust or degrade. The skewer is sized for medium-to-large parrots (conures through macaws) and can be loaded with enough fresh food for a full day's servings. For extra difficulty, interleave foraging-unfamiliar foods with known favorites to encourage exploration of the new items.

Pros:

  • Encourages fresh food acceptance — overcoming neophobia through novel food presentation
  • Stainless steel construction — hygienic, dishwasher-safe, won't rust
  • Combines feeding with enrichment — the bird works to access food, not just eats it
  • Reduces fresh food waste — birds eat more when food is presented as a puzzle
  • Adjustable hanging height encourages climbing and exercise
  • Holds a full day's fresh food portions
  • Suitable for medium to large parrots

Cons:

  • Not suitable for small birds (budgies, cockatiels) — prongs are spaced for larger food items
  • Fresh food must be cut into pieces that fit the prongs — adds prep time
  • Fruit juice can drip onto the cage floor during eating — requires cleaning below the skewer
  • Some birds ignore the skewer entirely if they're not food-motivated to manipulate it
  • Stainless steel is cold to the touch — some birds are initially hesitant to approach it

Rating: 4/5 | Best For: Fresh food introduction, medium-to-large parrots, birds with picky eating habits

6. Bonka Bird Toys Krafting Box — Best DIY Foraging Kit

The Bonka Bird Toys Krafting Box isn't a toy itself — it's a kit of foraging materials that lets you create custom foraging challenges for your bird. The box contains: 50 unluted small cardboard boxes (perfect size for hiding single almonds or walnuts), 50 feet of cotton twine for tying boxes to cage bars, 100 feet of crinkle paper for wrapping treats (Level 1 foraging), 25 bamboo skewers for threading food, 5 small paper lunch bags, 2 sheets of corrugated cardboard, and a bag of clean, heat-treated pine shavings for creating digging boxes. Think of it as a foraging supplies subscription in a one-time box.

With this kit, you can create hundreds of different foraging challenges: hide nuts in paper boxes wrapped in crinkle paper, create a foraging chain (tie 5 boxes together in sequence), make a paper bag "pinata" filled with treats and crumpled paper, or create a digging box (a tray of pine shavings with treats buried underneath). The Krafting Box removes the barrier of acquiring individual materials and gives you proven foraging designs with instructions. The cardboard and paper are safe for birds to shred and ingest in small amounts, and the cotton twine is safe if nibbled (unlike synthetic strings that can cause crop impaction).

Pros:

  • Endless variety — hundreds of unique foraging challenges from one kit
  • All materials are bird-safe, non-toxic, and consumable
  • Lets you match foraging difficulty exactly to your bird's skill level
  • Encourages creative engagement from the owner — foraging becomes a daily activity you design
  • Works for any bird species — adjust the size of boxes and wraps to match

7. Caitec Bamboo Foraging Tower — Best Heavy-Duty Puzzle for Large Parrots

The Caitec Bamboo Foraging Tower is a tiered wooden tower with multiple foraging compartments that large parrots must work through sequentially. The tower has three levels: the bottom level holds a shredding challenge (paper or palm leaf to destroy), the middle level requires turning a wooden knob to release food from a hopper, and the top level has a sliding drawer that opens to reveal a food reward. The bird must solve each level in order — the top-level drawer won't open until the bottom and middle challenges are addressed. This sequential requirement ensures extended engagement time — most large parrots spend 20-40 minutes working through all three levels.

The tower is made from bamboo (harder and denser than birch or pine) with stainless steel hardware — designed specifically for the destructive power of large macaws and cockatoos. The base is weighted and has a non-slip bottom that prevents the tower from tipping over during vigorous manipulation.   • Bamboo construction with stainless steel hardware — survives heavy chewers

8. The Original Foraging Buddy — Best Foraging Swing for Exercise + Enrichment

The Foraging Buddy combines two essential bird activities — swinging/movement and foraging — into a single toy. It's a rope and wood swing with a central foraging compartment that holds treats. Building a Foraging Routine: How to Integrate Foraging Toys Into Daily Feeding

Building a daily foraging routine for your parrot

  • Replace Bowl-Feeding with Foraging Feeding Gradually: Don't remove your bird's food bowl and replace it entirely with foraging toys on day one. This causes stress and potential weight loss. Instead, start by providing 10% of the daily diet through foraging toys while keeping 90% in the bowl. Over 2-4 weeks, gradually increase the foraging proportion and decrease the bowl proportion until the bird is working for 40-60% of its daily food intake through foraging. The remaining 40-60% should still be available in a bowl to ensure the bird never goes hungry — hungry birds become stressed birds, and stressed birds don't forage effectively
  • Rotate Toys to Prevent Habituation: Parrots are intelligent creatures that habituate to repeated stimuli. A foraging toy that takes 30 minutes to solve on day 1 takes 30 seconds by day 7 once the bird has memorized the solution. Maintain 4-6 foraging toys in rotation: two available at any time, two in cleaning/storage, and two being cycled back in after a 2-week break. Rotate toys every 3-4 days. When a previously solved toy returns after a 2-week absence, most birds approach it as a novel challenge and spend significant time re-exploring the puzzle
  • Use High-Value Rewards for Difficult Toys: Not all food is equally motivating. Seeds (sunflower, safflower), nuts (almonds, pine nuts, walnuts), and dried fruit (papaya, mango, banana chips) are high-value rewards that motivate foraging behavior. Pellets are low-value — birds will work harder for high-value items. Load new or difficult foraging toys with high-value rewards to establish the association between the toy and the reward. Once the bird is reliably using the toy, you can partially fill it with pellets and reserve high-value items for the final, most difficult compartment
  • Morning Foraging is Most Effective: Parrots are most food-motivated in the morning after an overnight fast. Schedule the most challenging foraging toys for the morning feeding session. Simpler foraging (including easy shredding toys and forage-able fresh food skewers) can be used for the evening meal. This pattern mirrors natural parrot behavior: wild parrots spend the morning hours foraging for the majority of their daily food intake and taper off foraging activity in the afternoon
  • Monitor for Frustration: Foraging should challenge, not frustrate. Signs of foraging frustration include: screaming at the toy (not at you — targeted screaming at the toy), throwing the toy aggressively, abandoning the toy after 2-3 attempts, or redirecting aggression toward cage mates or owners. If you see these signs, the foraging toy is too difficult. Step the bird back to Level 1 or 2 foraging for 1-2 weeks to rebuild confidence, then reintroduce harder toys. A parrot that experiences repeated failure at foraging will develop learned helplessness — the belief that effort doesn't lead to reward — which is the opposite of what foraging enrichment aims to achieve
  • Count Foraging Time as Cage Time Quality: Experts recommend that parrots should spend 30-60 minutes per day engaged in active foraging to meet their behavioral needs. This breaks down to: 15-20 minutes of morning high-difficulty forging (Level 3-4), 10-15 minutes of midday moderate foraging (Level 2), and 10-15 minutes of evening easy foraging (Level 1). Track your foraging time for the first week to ensure your bird is getting adequate enrichment — most owners discover that their birds are getting 5-10 minutes of foraging per day, not 30-60 minutes

FAQ

How many foraging toys does my parrot need?

As a starting point, provide one foraging toy per 100 grams of body weight — a 400-gram African grey needs 4 foraging toys in rotation; a 1000-gram macaw needs 8-10. Most parrot owners dramatically underestimate how much enrichment their birds need. If you have room in the cage for 2 foraging toys at a time, rotate between 4-6 toys on a schedule. If you have a dedicated play stand, add 2-4 more foraging stations there. Foraging toys should cover at least 30 minutes of active engagement per day. If your bird solves all available toys in under 10 minutes, you need more toys or harder toys.

My bird is afraid of new foraging toys. What do I do?

Parrots are neophobic by nature — fear of new objects is a survival instinct. Introduce new foraging toys slowly: place the toy near the cage (not inside) for 2-3 days so the bird can observe it from a safe distance. Then move the toy onto the cage top for 2-3 days. Then place it inside the cage near a favorite perch. The first time the toy is inside the cage, don't put food in it — let the bird investigate without the pressure of needing to retrieve food. On the third day of in-cage presence, place a single high-value treat (pine nut, sunflower seed) in the easiest possible location. If the bird retrieves it, the ice is broken — gradually increase the difficulty over the following week. Never force a bird to interact with a foraging toy — the choice to engage must be the bird's decision.

Can I make my own foraging toys instead of buying them?

Absolutely — DIY foraging is cost-effective and provides endless variety. Safe materials include: cardboard egg cartons (hide treats inside, close the lid), paper towel tubes (fold ends in, hide treats inside, poke a few holes for investigation), clean pine cones (wedge nuts between scales), small cardboard boxes from shipping (remove tape and staples, fill with crinkle paper and treats), paper lunch bags (fill with shredded paper and treats, twist the top closed), and untreated wooden blocks (drill holes and wedge nuts in). AVOID: tape of any kind (adhesive is toxic if ingested), staples or paperclips (sharp edges), glue (most glues are toxic), treated or dyed paper (inks can be toxic), and synthetic strings or ribbons (can cause crop impaction). When in doubt, use only plain cardboard, untreated paper, and natural wood.

Conclusion

Best bird foraging toys and puzzle feeders for 2026

For birds new to foraging and owners looking for the perfect introduction, the Planet Pleasures Foraging Wheel at $8-15 is an inexpensive, intuitive starter toy that teaches the fundamental concept of foraging — work produces food — in a way that birds of all sizes understand immediately.

For experienced foragers who need multi-step cognitive challenges, the Nina Ottosson Puzzle Box at $25-35 provides three different locking mechanisms in a single durable birch wood toy that exercises planning, sequencing, and fine motor control — the gold standard in intermediate-to-advanced puzzles.

For owners of large, destructive parrots (macaws, cockatoos, large Amazons) who need toys that survive powerful beaks, the Super Bird Creations Foraging Box at $20-30 uses replaceable shredding inserts inside an indestructible acrylic shell — the bird satisfies its destructive drive without destroying the toy itself.

For owners who want unlimited foraging variety at the lowest cost, the Bonka Bird Toys Krafting Box at $20-30 provides materials for hundreds of unique foraging challenges — paper, cardboard, twine, and skewers that let you design daily foraging puzzles at whatever difficulty level matches your bird.

For large macaws and cockatoos who need serious engagement, the Caitec Bamboo Foraging Tower at $35-50 offers a three-level sequential bamboo puzzle that provides 20-40 minutes of engagement — the most physically and cognitively demanding foraging toy available for large parrots.

And for every parrot, regardless of species or size, the ATOM Foraging Pinecone at $10-15 for a 6-pack provides the most natural foraging experience possible — wedging treats between pinecone scales mimics the wild foraging behavior that every parrot's brain is wired to perform.

The single most impactful change you can make for your parrot's welfare today is this: stop putting all of your bird's food in a bowl. Move 40-60% of the daily diet into foraging toys. The behavioral improvement you'll see within 2-4 weeks — reduced screaming, less feather destruction, increased activity, improved feather condition — will convince you that foraging enrichment isn't optional. It's the difference between a parrot that survives in captivity and a parrot that thrives.
